ref-title.ref-titletext-english: 1. Establishes general threat. 2. Asserts personal expertise as objective if reluctant ethnographer. 3. Gives convincing detail of witches' local organization

author.ce:initials: Gives convincing detail of witches' local organization .

reference.ref-fulltext: 1. Establishes general threat. 2. Asserts personal expertise as objective if reluctant ethnographer. 3. Gives convincing detail of witches' local organization .
reference.ce:source-text: 1. Establishes general threat. 2. Asserts personal expertise as objective if reluctant ethnographer. 3. Gives convincing detail of witches' local organization .

ref-title.ref-titletext-english: 1. Dated offence. 2. Observation of specific acts of deviance

author.ce:surname: Beyond the Great Story: History as Text and Discourse (Cambridge
Fiction in the Archives: Pardon Tales and their Tellers in Sixteenth-Century France (Stanford
'Reporting Murder: Fiction in the Archives in Early Modern England'

ref-info.ref-sourcetitle: Beyond the Great Story: History as Text and Discourse
ref-info.ref-text: (Cambridge, MA, 1995). 2. Natalie Zemon Davis, Fiction in the Archives: Pardon Tales and their Tellers in Sixteenth-Century France (Stanford, CA, 1987); Malcolm Gaskill, 'Reporting Murder: Fiction in the Archives in Early Modern England', Social History, 23 (1998) 1-30. 3. Diane Purkiss, The Witch in History: Early Modern and Twentieth-Century Representations (London, 1996), p. 75. 4. P. Spierenburg, The Spectacle of Suffering: Executions and the Evolution of Repression: From a Preindustrial Metropolis to the European Experience

reference.ref-fulltext: 1. Robert F. Berkhofer, Beyond the Great Story: History as Text and Discourse (Cambridge, MA, 1995). 2. Natalie Zemon Davis, Fiction in the Archives: Pardon Tales and their Tellers in Sixteenth-Century France (Stanford, CA, 1987); Malcolm Gaskill, 'Reporting Murder: Fiction in the Archives in Early Modern England', Social History, 23 (1998) 1-30. 3. Diane Purkiss, The Witch in History: Early Modern and Twentieth-Century Representations (London, 1996), p. 75. 4. P. Spierenburg, The Spectacle of Suffering: Executions and the Evolution of Repression: From a Preindustrial Metropolis to the European Experience
reference.ce:source-text: 1. Robert F. Berkhofer, Beyond the Great Story: History as Text and Discourse (Cambridge, MA, 1995). 2. Natalie Zemon Davis, Fiction in the Archives: Pardon Tales and their Tellers in Sixteenth-Century France (Stanford, CA, 1987); Malcolm Gaskill, 'Reporting Murder: Fiction in the Archives in Early Modern England', Social History, 23 (1998) 1-30. 3. Diane Purkiss, The Witch in History: Early Modern and Twentieth-Century Representations (London, 1996), p. 75. 4. P. Spierenburg, The Spectacle of Suffering: Executions and the Evolution of Repression: From a Preindustrial Metropolis to the European Experience
